nativeRange.habitat: Damp riparian zones along streams, rivers, canal banks, and waterlogged soils in temperate wetlands; reported from sea level up to about 500 m elevation

summary: Water Pepper (Persicaria hydropiper) is a temperate wetland stem plant that develops intense magenta-red coloration under strong light and CO2 injection. Most aquarium stock belongs to the cultivars 'Sao Paulo' or 'Kawagoeanum', both of which grow quickly and suit the background of planted tanks.
careNotes:
Water Pepper is a widespread temperate wetland herb known in botany as Persicaria hydropiper (the older name Polygonum hydropiper is still sometimes encountered). In the aquarium hobby it is most commonly sold as one of two cultivars — 'Sao Paulo' and 'Kawagoeanum' — though the unselected species form also appears occasionally. The plant is native across Europe, temperate Asia (including Japan, where it is called tade and used in traditional cuisine), North America, Australia, and New Zealand, growing along stream banks, canal margins, and waterlogged soils.

Good growth generally requires medium-to-high light output (T5 or quality LED), CO2 injection in the range of 20–40 mg/L, and a nutrient-rich substrate supported by regular iron and phosphate dosing. Under those conditions submersed stems tend to develop the intense magenta to wine-red coloration the plant is known for. In low-light tanks without CO2, growth tends to be slower and the foliage remains pale green rather than red.

Because the species originates from temperate climates, it is unusually tolerant of cooler water compared with most aquarium stem plants. It generally performs well across 18–28 °C, with optimal growth often noted around 23–27 °C, and can be a good choice for unheated or cool-running aquariums and paludariums. Water chemistry is flexible: a slightly acidic pH of 5.5–7.0 is preferred, and carbonate hardness anywhere from very soft to moderately hard (0–14 dKH) is broadly tolerated. Propagation is straightforward — trim the top portion of a mature stem and replant it in substrate; the remaining mother stem will typically produce lateral side-shoots.
breedingNotes: Water Pepper is propagated in the hobby by stem cuttings. Trim the top portion of a mature stem and replant it in nutrient-rich substrate; the remaining lower section will typically produce lateral side-shoots, effectively yielding two growing points from one stem. In the wild, the species is an annual herb that produces nodding flower spikes bearing small blossoms with four or five petals and reproduces by seed, but seed propagation is not practiced in aquarium keeping.

primary.name: Wikipedia
primary.notes: Promoted to primary after the designated Buce Plant URL (https://buceplant.com/products/persicaria-sp-kawagoeanum) returned 404. Used for canonical scientific name, family (Polygonaceae), order (Caryophyllales), basionym (Polygonum hydropiper), common-name synonyms (water pepper, marshpepper knotweed, tade), native range across temperate Northern and Southern hemispheres, and wild plant height (20-70 cm).

additional.name: Flowgrow
additional.notes: Cross-checked aquarium husbandry: medium-to-high light, CO2 20-40 mg/L, fast growth, mid/background placement, propagation by stem cuttings, temperature 18-30 °C (optimal 23-27 °C), pH 5-7, kH 0-14, medium care difficulty, iron and phosphate fertilization needs, magenta-red coloration under strong light. Documents the 'Sao Paulo' cultivar specifically — the most widely-traded form of the species in the hobby.
